title: MySQL Data Packing
text: MySQL Data Packing Search: Column Uniface Packing Code MySQL Server Storage Format Filter results... 46 rows found Table 1. Mappings Between Uniface Packing Codes and MySQL Storage Formats Uniface Packing Code MySQL Server Storage Format Filter 46 rows found Filter 46 rows found Uniface Packing Code MySQL Server Storage Format B1 Tinyint (Mapping 1) or Char (Mapping 2) B2 Tinyint (Mapping 1) or Char (Mapping 2) B3 Tinyint (Mapping 1) or Char (Mapping 2) B4 Tinyint B5 Tinyint C1-C255 Char C* Varchar D Date D1-D16 Date E Datetime E1-E16 Datetime F Double Precision F4 Float F8 Double Precision I1 Tinyint I2 Smallint I3 Mediumint I4 Int I8 Bigint J1-J32 Decimal M1-M8 Decimal N Int N1-N32 Decimal O1-O32 Decimal P1-P16 Decimal Q1-Q16 Decimal R1-R255 Binary R* Varbinary T Time T1, T2, T4-T8 Time T3 Datetime SC1-SC* Longtext (U4.0 ) or Varchar (U2.1 and U3.0) SR1-SR* Longblob (U4.0 ) or Varbinary (U2.1 and U3.0) SU1-SU* Longtext (U4.0 ) or Varchar (U2.1 and U3.0) SW1-SW* Longtext (U4.0 ) or Varchar (U2.1 and U3.0) U1-U255 Char U* Varchar VC1-VC* Varchar VR1-VR* Varbinary VU1-VU* Varchar VW1-VW* 1 Varchar W1-W255 1 Char W256-higher 1 Varchar W* 1 Varchar Y1-Y32 Binary Z1-Z8 Binary Mapping 1 and 2 The difference between mapping 1 and mapping 2 lies in how it treats boolean packing codes B1, B2, and B3. If a table has been created using a column with a packing code of B1, B2, or B3 in mapping 1, then a connector using mapping 2 will be unable to retrieve the rows written with mapping 1. To address this issue, you can do one of the following: Set the connector option mapping = 1 Export the table using the mapping = 1 , then import the table using mapping = 2 . For tables that have used the default B packing code, all values will be interpreted correctly. Segmented Fields The MQL U4.0 connector supports segmented fields. By default, packing codes with the prefix S (SC, SR, SU, SW) are mapped to large object data types. This can be disabled using the connector option segmented fields: disabled . In this case, Uniface uses the overflow tables mechanism. For more information, see segmented fields . For the MQL U2.1 and U3.0 connectors, the overflow table mechanism is used to support large data fields. The following Uniface packing codes require an overflow table when the amount of data to be inserted into the field exceeds 255 bytes: C* , SC* , VC* U*, SU* , VU* R*, SR*, VR* W*, SW*, VW* MySQL Storage Formats Table 2. MySQL Storage Formats Storage format Description Char The Char storage format is used to store fixed-length character strings. The storage size is equal to the length of the character string. Arithmetic with Char fields is impossible without first converting the data into a numerical format, even if the field contains only numeric data. Decimal The Decimal storage format is used to store unpacked fixed-point numbers. Decimal behaves like a Char column; the number is stored as a string using one character for each digit of the value. The maximum range of Decimal values is the same as for Double, but the actual range for a given Decimal column could be constrained by the choice of precision and scale. Tinyint The Tinyint storage format is used to store very small integers from -128 to 127 (or -27 to 27 -1) The storage size is 1 byte. Smallint The Smallint storage format is used to store small integers from -32768 to 32767 (or -215 to 215 -1). The storage size is 2 bytes. Mediumint The Mediumint storage format is used to store medium-sized integers from -8388608 to 8388607 (or -223 to 223 -1). The storage size is 3 bytes. Int The Int storage format is used to store normal integers from -2147483648 to 2147483647 (or -231 to 231 -1). The storage size is 4 bytes. Bigint The Bigint storage format is used to store large integers from -9223372036854775808 to 9223372036854775807 (or -263 to 263 -1). The storage size is 8 bytes. Float The Float storage format is used to store small single-precision floating-point values in the range -3.402823466E+38 to -1.175494351E-38, 0, and from 1.175494351E-38 to 3.402823466E+38. Storage size is 4 bytes if precision is between 0 and 24 digits. Double Precision The Double Precision (or Double) storage format is used to store floating point values from -1.7976931348623157E+308 to -2.2250738585072014E-308, 0, and 2.2250738585072014E-308 to 1.7976931348623157E+308. The precision of the Double data type is 15 digits. The storage size is 8 bytes. Datetime The Datetime storage format is used to store datetime values of the form YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS, and has a storage size of 16 bytes. The value of day field must be in the range from 1 to the number of days in the month. The number of days in the month is determined from the values of the year and month fields and is 28, 29, 30, or 31. The value of the month field must be in the range 1 through 12. The value of the year field must be in the range 0 through 9,999. Years are measured from 0 A.D. Some data sources do not support the entire range of years. The value of the hour field must be in the range 0 through 23. The value of the minute field must be in the range 0 through 59. The value of the second field must be in the range 0 through 59. Date The Date storage format is used to store date values of the form YYYY-MM-DD. The storage size is six bytes. Time The Time storage format is used to store time values of the form HH:MM:SS. The storage size is 6 bytes. Varchar Varchar storage format is used when the expected length of the data can vary considerably: in a name field for example, when the same field will be used to hold the values "JJB Inc." and "International Sub-Oceanic Refuse Engineering Corporation". The storage size of a Varchar field depends on its contents: if the field is empty, no space is used. Varchar fields, like Char fields, can be used for any combination of characters, numbers and symbols. Binary The Binary storage format is used to store fixed-length binary data. Varbinary The Varbinary storage format is used to store variable-length binary data. Use the Varbinary storage format when the length of your data can vary considerably. The storage size of a Varbinary field depends on its contents: if the field is empty, no space is used. Longtext Text values are treated as nonbinary strings (character strings). They have a character set, and values are sorted and compared based on the collation of the character set. Longblob Blob values are treated as binary strings (byte strings). They have no character set, and sorting and comparison are based on the numeric values of the bytes in column values. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
